mentors.name: The Mindset Architect
mentors.bio: A renowned strategist of inner game and peak performance. Coaches founders and executives on state, focus, and decisive action.
mentors.guidance_style: High-energy, pattern-interrupt, focuses on state and strategy

mentors.name: The Sales Commander
mentors.bio: A sales-training authority and operator who built a philosophy around massive action and ten-times growth.

mentors.name: The Decision Coach
mentors.bio: An expert on habit formation, decisive action, and overcoming self-doubt — a champion of small, immediate moves.

mentors.name: The Acquisition Operator
mentors.bio: A former institutional investor turned acquisition entrepreneur. Expert in finding and operating unsexy, cash-flowing businesses.

mentors.name: The Brand Pioneer
mentors.bio: A serial entrepreneur and adventurer who built a sprawling brand empire of hundreds of companies around people-first principles.

mentors.name: The Scale Strategist
mentors.bio: Built a telecom empire across emerging markets. Expert in scaling in resource-constrained environments.

mentors.name: The AI Innovator
mentors.bio: A leading AI product founder. Builds search-and-answer systems from first principles and disrupts established markets.

investors.name: The Mirror of Identity
investors.bio: An identity-first investor and probing brand thinker. Tests whether founders truly know themselves before testing the business.
investors.signature_question: Why are YOU the right person to build this business, and what makes you obsessed with solving this problem?
investors.walk_out_trigger: Identity unclear - founder doesn't know who they are or why they're doing this

investors.name: The Master of Coin
investors.bio: A cold, numbers-driven capital allocator. Famous for royalty deals and ruthless financial logic.
investors.signature_question: How does this business make money, and when will it become profitable?
investors.walk_out_trigger: Financial logic weak - numbers don't add up or founder can't explain unit economics

investors.name: The Lord of Hustle
investors.bio: A brand-from-nothing operator who turned street-style apparel into a global label. Bets on hustle and clear differentiation.
investors.signature_question: How will you build demand and make customers care about this brand?
investors.walk_out_trigger: Brand weak - no clear differentiation or brand strategy

investors.name: The Mother of Instinct
investors.bio: Built a real-estate empire from a thousand-dollar loan. Trusts gut instinct and bets on founder grit.
investors.signature_question: What did you learn the hard way while building this business?
investors.walk_out_trigger: Emotional authenticity weak - founder seems rehearsed or lacks genuine passion

investors.name: The Hand of Execution
investors.bio: A serial tech entrepreneur who sold an early internet venture for billions. Values product excellence and relentless execution.
investors.signature_question: How will you actually build and scale this product better than competitors?
investors.walk_out_trigger: Scalability weak - product or business model can't scale

investors.name: The Spider of Strategy
investors.bio: A prolific product inventor holding more than a hundred patents. Expert in product-market fit, retail strategy, and competitive moats.
investors.signature_question: What makes your solution clearly better than everything else in the market?
investors.walk_out_trigger: Clarity weak - founder can't clearly explain their product or strategy

investors.name: The Warden of Trust
investors.bio: An immigrant success story who built a cybersecurity empire. Values integrity and trust above all.
investors.signature_question: Why should I trust you beyond the numbers?
investors.walk_out_trigger: Trust weak - inconsistencies in story or founder seems untrustworthy

leaders.name: The Institution Builder
leaders.bio: A former chief executive of a global consumer-goods giant. Known for long-horizon, purpose-driven strategy.

leaders.name: The Conviction Founder
leaders.specialization: Grit, belief under doubt, unconventional thinking
leaders.bio: Rejected from thirty jobs, built one of the world's largest e-commerce platforms on grit and unconventional belief.

leaders.name: The Purpose Translator
leaders.bio: A leading voice on purpose-driven leadership. Expert in helping organizations find and articulate their why.

leaders.name: The Execution Mentor
leaders.bio: An authority on personal productivity, goal achievement, and the disciplined execution of priorities.

leaders.name: The Leadership Elder
leaders.bio: A foremost authority on the laws and stages of leadership maturity, character, and influence.

leaders.name: The Nation Builder
leaders.bio: Chairman of a multi-decade industrial conglomerate. Known for patient capital and a nation-building, long-game vision.

leaders.name: The Frugal Operator
leaders.bio: Founder and CEO of a bootstrapped software empire. Built to a billion in revenue without external funding — frugal, product-first, customer-first.

competencies.name: Problem Sensing
competencies.description: Does the founder deeply understand the customer's real pain?
what_it_measures: Solves meaningful problems, not surface inconveniences
Articulates emotional + financial cost of the problem
Obsessed with the problem before the solution
developing: Talks more about solution than customer pain
Cannot clearly articulate emotional + financial cost
Focuses on idea excitement over problem obsession
strong: Can clearly describe customer pain and stakes
Balances problem depth with solution development
advanced: Deeply obsessed with understanding root pain
Quantifies emotional and financial impact clearly
Anticipates adjacent and second-order customer problems
Builds solution from pain precision, not assumption

competencies.name: Learning Agility
competencies.description: Does the founder actively seek feedback and adapt?
what_it_measures: Willing to pivot based on learning
advanced: Evolves model ahead of market shifts

competencies.name: Courage to Commit
competencies.description: Can the founder make decisions with incomplete information?
developing: Hesitates to take or own risk
strong: Makes timely decisions with imperfect info

competencies.name: Financial Discipline
competencies.description: Does the founder understand and manage money wisely?
developing: Uses hiring to compensate for weak strategy
strong: Uses partnerships to offset cash constraints
Calculates equity trade against dilution impact

competencies.name: Strategic Thinking
competencies.description: Can the founder see the big picture beyond day-to-day operations?
what_it_measures: Sees big picture beyond daily ops
Understands how today's decisions impact 1-3 years ahead
Fixes root causes instead of patches
developing: Struggles to connect decisions to long-term impact
Reacts to problems instead of anticipating
strong: Pauses before reacting to external shocks

competencies.name: Power & Influence
competencies.description: Can the founder defend value and negotiate from strength?
developing: Accepts unfavorable terms to avoid discomfort
strong: Protects core terms while remaining flexible
advanced: Practices ABC mindset and takes quick win-win decisions

competencies.name: Team & Customer Management
competencies.description: Does the founder genuinely care about customer satisfaction and team well-being?
what_it_measures: Genuinely cares about customer satisfaction beyond transactions
Treats team as assets to grow, not exploit
Makes decisions with long-term trust in mind
Builds feedback loops for customers and employees
strong: Checks bias in hiring and promotions
advanced: Protects both customer and team equity under pressure
Prioritizes humanity over profits when demanded
Maintains psychological safety under performance pressure

competencies.name: Value Creation & Personal Credibility
competencies.description: Does the founder build lasting value and reputation?
what_it_measures: Considers impact on family, team, community
Builds brand equity, not just numbers
strong: Actively shares knowledge and educates customers
advanced: Operates as value creator first, revenue follows
Invests in community, team growth, and ecosystem
Builds authority through service and insight

competencies.name: Perseverance & Adaptability
competencies.description: Can the founder persist through setbacks and adapt strategy when needed?
what_it_measures: Adapts strategy without losing core direction
Stays committed to vision while pivoting tactics
developing: Gives up after initial failures or setbacks
Repeats the same failing strategy without adjustment
Denies or blames external factors for the crisis
Reacts emotionally to setbacks instead of analyzing
strong: Recognizes mistakes in previous decisions and adjusts
Responds calmly to setbacks and pressure
Adjusts spending or strategy to stabilize the business
advanced: Quickly reframes setbacks as feedback and adapts
Adapts strategy, capital allocation, and priorities to recover
Maintains composure and conviction under sustained pressure
Demonstrates anti-fragility — gets stronger from adversity

stages.name: Ideation
stages.title: Stage -2: IDEATION
stages.goal: Can the participant identify important problems, understand the customer or stakeholder involved, and propose solutions that create meaningful value?
stages.research_background: McKinsey & Company – Leadership Skills Research (2023) says Organizations increasingly promote leaders who can: identify problems early, understand root causes, design strategic solutions

proficiency_rubric.P1: The participant chooses ideas based on trends, hype, or copying others. Customer and problem are vague or broad. Value is unclear or generic.
proficiency_rubric.P2: The participant shows basic understanding of the customer problem and the market. They can identify a customer group and describe a practical value proposition.
proficiency_rubric.P3: The participant demonstrates deep problem awareness and strong value logic. They clearly connect customer pain → solution → differentiation.

questions.text: What problem are you solving in one sentence?
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Evaluate clarity, specificity, and problem-focus of this business idea statement. P3 if the problem is specific, urgent, and clearly affecting a defined group.

questions.text: What are you selling first?

options.text: A service
options.signal: Service-based, trades time for money initially

options.text: A product
A Technology
Subscription
options.signal: Recurring revenue model, needs retention focus

options.text: Marketplace / platform
Others (one line)

questions.text: How did this business idea originate for you?

options.text: I experienced this problem personally and wanted to solve it
I saw many people struggling with this problem and believed it needed a solution
I researched the market and saw a strong opportunity
I noticed others making good money in this space
I saw the trend growing on social media and wanted to explore it
I want financial independence and believe this business can achieve that
Other (one sentence)

questions.text: Who exactly is your target customer? (Choose one primary group only)

options.text: Students
Working professionals
Business owners
Parents
A specific niche (write 1 sentence)

questions.text: What industry does your business operate in? (Choose one primary)

options.text: Education
Health / Wellness
Food / Consumer Goods
Technology / Software
Media / Content
Services (consulting, coaching, agency, etc.)
Marketplace / Platform
Other (one sentence)

questions.text: Without which of the following will you NOT enter a business:

options.text: Knowledge of the industry and how the business works
Prior experience
Enough money to build the business
A perfect product ready before launch
Experts to run the business for you
Strong partners who can handle operations for you

questions.text: When do customers look for your solution, and why do they choose it?

options.text: They waste too much time, and my solution makes the process faster or easier
They get poor quality, and my solution delivers better quality outcomes
They are losing money, and my solution provides a more affordable option
They feel overwhelmed or confused, and my solution simplifies the process
They cannot find a reliable option, and my solution focuses deeply on this problem
A combination of cost, quality, and time improvements
Customers may not clearly realize the problem yet, but I believe this solution will become important
I believe customers will choose it because it is new, innovative, or interesting
Other (one short sentence)

questions.text: What is the specific domain or industry of your business?
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Evaluate clarity of the chosen domain/industry.

questions.text: Is the core of your business idea primarily technical (e.g., deep software engineering, AI, complex tech infrastructure)?

options.text: Yes, it is a technical startup
No, it is primarily non-technical (operations, physical product, services, basic e-commerce)

stages.name: Vision
stages.title: Stage -1: Vision
stages.goal: Whether the participant can think strategically about direction, focus, and long-term value creation. Define long-term direction.
stages.research_background: McKinsey & Company – Leadership capability research (2023) says Companies perform better when leaders can: set a clear direction, align teams around a vision, anticipate market changes
what_happens: Identify the problem that the business is solving
Describe what success looks like in 3 years

proficiency_rubric.P1: The participant thinks short-term or unfocused. They expand too broadly or chase growth without clarity.
proficiency_rubric.P2: The participant shows clear strategic thinking about growth and value. They focus on solving an important problem and building a clear market position.
proficiency_rubric.P3: The participant demonstrates system-level thinking and long-term scalability.

questions.text: Your idea could solve several problems for customers. How would you launch the business and what would success look like after 3 years?

options.text: Start by solving one important problem extremely well for a specific customer group, build a trusted solution, and expand capabilities once strong product-market fit is achieved.
Solve several related customer problems so the product becomes broadly useful and attracts a growing user base.
Launch with a product that includes multiple useful features and continue improving it as customer usage grows.
Launch quickly, learn from early users, and expand into new opportunities as the business discovers where demand is strongest.
Design the business early with strong systems, processes, and technology so it can scale efficiently across markets over time.

questions.text: What does success look like for this business in 3 years?

options.text: A product used by a growing number of customers with new features added over time to expand its capabilities.
A well-recognized solution within a specific market that consistently solves an important problem for a defined customer group.
A scalable business built around strong systems, processes, and technology that can grow across multiple markets while maintaining value for customers.
A business that continues to explore adjacent opportunities and expand into new areas as it learns from customers and the market.

questions.text: What meaningful outcome will your solution create for customers?

options.text: Improve existing solutions by adding useful features that enhance the experience
Introduce a new approach and evolve the value as we learn from customers
Build strong adoption first and refine value as the business grows
Solve a clearly defined customer problem in a way that delivers measurably better results than current alternatives

questions.text: As the business grows and technology evolves over the next 3 years, how would you design the company to scale?

options.text: Focus on building systems and using technologies like AI to scale operations and improve customer experience
Improve the product gradually and add technology when necessary
Continue with the current approach since technology may not significantly affect this business
Focus on growth first and figure out systems and operations later

questions.text: Your product vision is clear, now you must decide how the product will actually be delivered to customers as you grow. What fulfillment strategy will you pursue?

options.text: Hire experts to design and manage fulfillment operations.
Partner with logistics/service providers who already have fulfillment infrastructure.
Run fulfillment yourself first to understand the system, then hire or partner to scale.

questions.text: What is your plan to get more customers?

options.text: Cold outreach - email/DM
Run paid ads on social media
Leverage personal network and ask for referrals
Partner with complementary businesses for cross-promotion
Create valuable social media content to attract inbound leads
Offer a pilot program or trial
Partner with an existing community
Others - one short sentence

stages.name: Commitment
stages.title: Stage 0: Commitment
stages.goal: Whether the participant can commit resources responsibly under uncertainty and make financial trade-offs.
stages.research_background: Deloitte – Global Human Capital and leadership capability research says Effective leaders must be able to make decisions with limited resources and uncertainty, particularly when managing budgets or investments.
what_happens: First Budget Allocation with runway awareness
Makes decisions based on budget priorities

proficiency_rubric.P1: The participant avoids risk or makes emotion-driven financial decisions. Choosing options without considering long-term consequences.
proficiency_rubric.P2: The participant balances risk and opportunity while maintaining reasonable financial discipline.
proficiency_rubric.P3: The participant demonstrates entrepreneurial capital strategy and resource leverage. Allocates capital strategically for growth and scalability.

questions.text:
GENERATE CAPITAL:
You are about to start your business and an ideal capital needed is $50,000.

You currently have $0 available to invest.

Your goal is to generate your FIRST $1 of capital to start the business. What approach do you take?

options.text: Wait until you personally save enough money - You decide to delay the launch until you accumulate some savings. You will save the goal amount of $50,000 in 24 months.
Take a small personal loan - You take a loan believing you can repay it once the business starts generating revenue. 10% interest - $1,200/month
options.signal: Willing to take financial risk, has obligation

options.text: Ask a friend or family member to invest - Someone close to you is willing to put in money in exchange for a small ownership stake. 10–15% equity
Find a partner who can invest capital - You bring in a co-founder primarily because they can fund the initial costs. 20–30% equity
Offer revenue-share to someone with capital - Instead of giving equity, you propose a revenue-share arrangement. 15% of revenue until $75K repaid
Collect pre-orders from early customers and do the job by yourself until you know. Early customers made pre-orders worth $50,000
Partner with an existing business for distribution - You collaborate with a business that already serves your target customers and launch through them. 25% distribution fee
Launch a crowdfunding campaign - You present the idea publicly and try to raise capital from many small contributors.
You launch a very small version of the business, do everything yourself and reinvest the revenue until you reach the $50,000 needed to grow.

questions.text: Generates $50,000 Capital
questions.context_text: Based on your capital generation strategy, you have successfully raised $50,000 in startup capital. You now need to decide how to allocate this budget.

questions.text:
First Budget Allocation
You now have $50,000 in startup capital. You must decide where the first major allocation goes.
Where do you allocate first?

options.text: Product Development
Marketing / Customer Acquisition
Creditors payback
Hiring a full-time team member to support you
Operations (Systems / Tools / Transport etc.)
Emergency

warnings.hiring_gt_40: WARNING: >40% on hiring before PMF = risky
warnings.emergency_lt_5: WARNING: No emergency buffer could derail you
warnings.product_lt_20: WARNING: Low product budget = quality issues

questions.text: Checkpoint: Do you want to continue with this business plan, or restart from Month 0 to refine your idea?

options.text: Continue with current plan
Restart from Month 0 (Ideation)
options.signal: Learning agility - choosing to pivot early

stages.name: Validation
stages.title: Stage 1: Validation
stages.goal: Can the participant interpret customer reactions and decide actions that improve product value and customer satisfaction, while staying committed to the vision and value?
stages.research_background: Harvard Research shows that leaders who can interpret customer feedback and adapt their actions improve customer satisfaction, retention, and revenue.
what_happens: AI introduces customer problems — Low traction, churn, complaints, refund requests, or poor reviews

proficiency_rubric.P1: Misinterprets or ignores customer reactions. Reacts emotionally or defensively to negative feedback. Focuses on quick fixes instead of understanding root causes. Blames customers, market, or marketing rather than improving the product.
proficiency_rubric.P2: Correctly interprets customer reactions and feedback. Adjusts product or customer acquisition strategies logically. Addresses customer concerns while protecting the core value.
proficiency_rubric.P3: Quickly identifies the root cause of customer dissatisfaction. Makes strategic improvements that increase customer value and traction. Maintains strong alignment with the original vision.

questions.text: Business Dashboard
questions.context_text: AI shows Dashboard of Leaders, Capital remaining, Revenue, Profit vs Expense and runway based on Stage 0 Budget allocation. Simulates sales, revenue numbers, customer count, customer churn, and predicts valuation.

questions.text: Dynamic Scenario 1
Dynamic Scenario 2

stages.name: Growth
stages.title: Stage 2: Growth
stages.goal: Can the participant manage financial pressure created by earlier decisions, balancing runway protection, strategic trade-offs, and adaptability when the business faces capital constraints?
stages.research_background: Financial resilience in startups depends on managing cash flow, runway, and strategic trade-offs during growth phases.
what_happens: Capital pressure and cash flow issues emerge
Participant analyzes the cause & reallocates budget
Trade-offs between survival and growth are tested

proficiency_rubric.P1: Confuses revenue with cash flow. Allocates emotionally. Ignores runway or creditor obligations. Makes isolated decisions without considering consequences.
proficiency_rubric.P2: Protects runway and prioritizes essential spending. Balances growth with financial stability. Considers trade-offs before allocating capital.
proficiency_rubric.P3: Optimizes capital allocation strategically. Protects runway, manages obligations, and allocates capital to highest ROI activities. Anticipates second-order effects.

questions.text:
Second Budget Allocation
You are in Month 5 of your business. Let's work on the budget for the next FIVE months.
Where do you allocate first?

options.text: Product Development
Marketing / Customer Acquisition
Creditors payback
Hiring full-time team members as business is growing
Operations (Systems / Tools / Transport etc.)
Emergency

questions.text: Dynamic Scenario 1
Dynamic Scenario 2

stages.name: Growth (Team)
stages.title: Stage 2: Growth (Part 2)
stages.goal: Can the participant manage team and partner challenges under pressure while maintaining performance, morale, and stakeholder trust?
stages.research_background: Research shows that leaders who effectively manage internal teams and external stakeholders improve organizational performance, trust, and long-term strategic outcomes.
what_happens: Fire or replace a team member
Stakeholders apply pressure to get more profit
pressure_injected: Need to fire a team member to protect performance
Business partner not delivering expected results

proficiency_rubric.P1: Avoids difficult decisions such as firing underperforming team members. Reacts emotionally when a key employee quits. Makes reactive decisions that reduce trust.
proficiency_rubric.P2: Addresses team performance issues directly. Responds calmly when a key team member quits. Manages partner expectations while protecting business direction.
proficiency_rubric.P3: Handles team exits, conflict, and stakeholder pressure with composure and clarity. Quickly stabilizes the team. Uses influence and communication to maintain trust.

questions.text: Dynamic Scenario 1
Dynamic Scenario 2

stages.name: Scale
stages.title: Stage 3: Scale
stages.goal: This stage evaluates whether participants can choose the right growth path under pressure, balancing power, strategy, courage, and adaptability when faced with partnerships, buyouts, and AI-driven disruption.
stages.research_background: Scaling requires strategic decision-making around partnerships, equity preservation, and adopting disruptive technologies like AI.
what_happens: Business performance is revealed based on earlier decisions (growing, stable, or struggling)
AI automation is introduced as a scaling opportunity
Competitors or investors try to influence company control
Participant decides how to grow while protecting leverage, ownership, and long-term direction
pressure_injected: AI automation threatening to replace parts of the business

proficiency_rubric.P1: Accepts partnerships or buyout offers that weaken control. Focuses on short-term gains. Gives up leverage quickly.
proficiency_rubric.P2: Evaluates partnerships, buyouts, and AI opportunities carefully. Protects equity and business value while negotiating.
proficiency_rubric.P3: Negotiates from strength and protects equity while maximizing long-term value. Anticipates strategic impact. Walks from bad offers.

questions.text: Dynamic Scenario 1
Strategic Crossroads: Do you accept a buyout offer or enter the War Room to raise capital and scale independently?

options.text: AGREE TO BUY OUT DEAL - Sell the business now and exit.
WALK OUT OF THE DEAL & ENTER WAR ROOM - Pitch to investors for growth capital.

stages.name: War Room Prep
stages.title: WAR ROOM PREP
stages.goal: Prepare for WAR ROOM — practice pitch and negotiation strategy
stages.research_background: Synthesis and preparation are key to successful high-stakes pitching and negotiation.

questions.text:
Fill in the Pitch Template:

Hello Sharks, my name is [NAME] and I am the founder of [BUSINESS].

(The Problem) Today, [TARGET CUSTOMER] struggles with [PROBLEM]. This problem causes them [IMPACT].

(The Solution) I created [PRODUCT], which [VALUE PROP]. It works by [HOW].

(Why We're Different) Unlike [COMPETITORS], we [DIFFERENTIATION].

(Proof) We validated this by [VALIDATION]. So far, we have [TRACTION].

(Founder Fit) I am building this because [WHY ME]. The key lesson I've learned is [LESSON].

(The Ask) We are raising $[AMOUNT] for [EQUITY]% equity. We will use this capital to [PLAN].
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Evaluate pitch completeness, clarity, confidence, financial logic, and overall narrative strength. P3 if the pitch is clear, compelling, and shows strong strategic thinking.

stages.name: War Room
stages.title: Stage 4 Final: War Room
stages.goal: The stated goal is to win a deal. The evaluation focuses on the ability to defend value, negotiate intelligently, and protect long-term strategy. Walking away from a bad deal shows strong entrepreneurial judgment.
what_happens: Participant may secure a deal or not
Participant can agree or deny a deal

proficiency_rubric.P1: Struggles to defend the business value or strategy. Accepts weak deals or gives up control too quickly. Short-term focus.
proficiency_rubric.P2: Evaluates deal terms logically before deciding. Negotiates to protect equity. Accepts or rejects deals based on clear reasoning.
proficiency_rubric.P3: Accepts a deal that strongly supports business goals OR walks away from a bad deal. Demonstrates conviction and strategic negotiation discipline.

questions.text: Deliver your 1-minute War Room pitch using the template you prepared.
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Evaluate pitch completeness, clarity, confidence, financial logic, and overall narrative strength. This is the final integrated assessment.

questions.text: Investor Panel Questions
questions.context_text:
Each selected investor asks their signature question based on their personality:

The Mirror of Identity: 'Why are YOU the right person to build this business, and what makes you obsessed with solving this problem?'
The Master of Coin: 'How does this business make money, and when will it become profitable?'
The Lord of Hustle: 'How will you build demand and make customers care about this brand?'
The Mother of Instinct: 'What did you learn the hard way while building this business?'
The Hand of Execution: 'How will you actually build and scale this product better than competitors?'
The Spider of Strategy: 'What makes your solution clearly better than everything else in the market?'
The Warden of Trust: 'Why should an investor trust you to build this business long-term?'
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Each of the 4 selected investors asks their signature question. Evaluate the participant's responses for confidence, depth, and alignment with each investor's primary lens.

questions.text: Deal Offers & Negotiation
questions.context_text:
Based on investor scorecards and business performance:
• DEAL 1 — High equity deal: One offer and one counter-offer
• DEAL 2 — Low investment deal: One offer and one counter-offer

Participant can accept, reject, or counter each deal.
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Generate two deal offers based on business performance dashboard. Evaluate negotiation strategy, equity protection, and deal-structuring ability.

questions.text: Listen to counter offer & Negotiate
questions.context_text: Investors respond with counter-offers. Participant must decide whether to accept, counter, or walk away.
questions.ai_eval_prompt: Evaluate negotiation composure, ability to protect value, and strategic deal-making under pressure.

questions.text: Final Decision: Do you accept or reject the deal?

options.text: Accept the deal
Reject the deal and walk away
options.signal: Conviction — evaluate if walking away was strategic
